Identifying RR(PF)(x) from OR(P)(x) relies critically on Assumption 6 (Noncase Exchangeability), which requires that the probability of symptomatic non-SARS-CoV-2 illness conditional on covariates X is identical for exposed (A=1) and unexposed (A=0) individuals (PF(Y=0, D=1|A=1, X) = PF(Y=0, D=1|A=0, X)). If the vaccine cross-protects against non-COVID illnesses, noncase exchangeability is violated (PF(Y=0, D=1|A=1, X) < PF(Y=0, D=1|A=0, X)), causing the observed odds ratio OR(P)(x) to underestimate vaccine protection against COVID-19 and biasing the estimated vaccine effectiveness downward.

Removing kernel localization and fitting a global PL-DML model forces a single constant slope across all penetration levels, averaging out the strong price reductions at low penetration (up to 7 GBP/MWh per GWh) and the near-zero marginal effects at mid-penetration (20-30%).

ground_truth.answer_text: In the GPI framework, causal identification requires disentangling the pre-specified treatment feature from other confounding features present in the LLM's internal representation vector. The separability assumption guarantees that the treatment feature is not a deterministic function of confounding features (and vice versa), ensuring that one can hypothetically intervene on the treatment feature without altering background confounding features. If separability is violated, changing the treatment feature deterministically shifts confounding features generated by the LLM, entangling the direct effect of the treatment with changes in confounders and causing severe overlap violations where certain confounder representations occur exclusively under one treatment status.

ground_truth.answer_text: In structured non-i.i.d. settings such as spatial processes or single networks, standard causal identification fails because observing a single realization of dependent units provides insufficient empirical degrees of freedom to consistently estimate nonparametric counterfactual distributions across units. Assuming that the causal mechanisms are equivariant under an amenable symmetry group G enables ergodic theory to hold across orbits of the group action, ensuring that averages over transformed indices converge to spatial or network ensemble expectations. Without group symmetry, interference across units introduces non-identifiable unit-specific confounding and propagation pathways, making consistent nonparametric estimation of causal effects from a single realization mathematically impossible.

ground_truth.answer_text: First-order influence function estimators rely on a von Mises linearization whose second-order remainder term decays at rate O(n^{-2s/d}), which dominates the parametric n^{-1/2} rate whenever s/d \le 0.5. By constructing higher-order influence functions using U-statistics to estimate and subtract leading bias terms of the von Mises expansion up to order m, HOIF debiased estimators reduce the remaining bias to O(n^{-(m+1)s/d}). ground_truth.answer_text: Standard offline reward models in APO evaluate prompt effectiveness correlationally, confounding prompt performance with query difficulty since easier queries naturally yield higher success rates across all prompts (Chen et al., 2602.01711). CPO solves this by applying Double Machine Learning to continuous semantic embedding representations of prompts and queries, orthogonalizing query-specific confounding to isolate the true causal average treatment effect of prompt perturbations. Gare et al. (2606.03251) provide the structural graph foundation for this phenomenon by demonstrating that real-world datasets harbor implicit natural experiments—subgroup interventions where causal graph feature selection separates true interventional drivers from spurious observational correlations. By leveraging causal rather than correlational feature representations, both CPO and causal feature selection prevent models from exploiting brittle surface correlations, dramatically improving out-of-distribution robustness on difficult reasoning queries.

ground_truth.answer_text: Standard DML residualization operates on scalar or low-dimensional continuous treatments, which fails when treatments are high-dimensional categorical combinations such as competing team formations because indicator residualization ignores inter-category structural restrictions (Ruiz-Menárguez & Badiella, 2602.16830). When extending DML to discover continuous effect modification—such as Shan's (2604.19066) finding of eroding physical activity protection among young adults—combining DML orthogonal residuals with Causal Forests isolates treatment effect heterogeneity from confounding age gradients. ground_truth.answer_text: Plecko demonstrates that while standard predictive classifiers learn a single conditional mechanism f_{\widehat Y} and inherit real-world causal mechanisms for all covariates, generative models implicitly construct and substitute their own internal causal mechanisms across all variables. ground_truth.answer_text: Optimal policy tree learning recursively partitions Euclidean covariate space via axis-aligned splits that maximize welfare or treatment effectiveness based on estimated CATEs, whereas CaSPECT constructs a causal DAG and embeds units via Chung's directed Laplacian so clusters reflect topological causal propagation pathways. When causal heterogeneity is driven by multi-variable causal dependencies rather than simple covariate thresholds, shallow policy trees can merge causally distinct units or overfit, whereas CaSPECT uncovers structurally homogeneous subpopulations without pre-specifying a propensity score model. Conversely, policy trees output global, interpretable decision rules directly mapping observed covariates to discrete treatment allocations, whereas CaSPECT identifies topologically coherent subpopulations that require subsequent within-cluster effect estimation.

ground_truth.answer_text: In Armendariz and Huber (2026), testing effect homogeneity and unobserved confounding across observational and experimental panel settings under difference-in-differences relies on standard parallel trends to locally identify the conditional Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATET) via double machine learning orthogonal score functions. However, when treated cohorts exhibit differential pre-treatment trends, standard parallel trends (Parallel[1]) is violated, causing standard ATET estimands to be biased and leading DML score tests to confound true treatment effect heterogeneity with differential pre-trend bias. Anteneh (2026) resolves this by replacing Parallel[1] with a hierarchy of higher-order conditions Parallel[p], which require only that the p-th order time difference of the untreated outcome gap is common across groups. Furthermore, when cohorts differ in their pre-treatment window lengths and support distinct polynomial orders p_g, Anteneh's Theorem 4.4 proves point identification for coherent weighted averages of group-time ATTs across cohort-heterogeneous orders without discarding cohorts.

ground_truth.answer_text: Under treatment effect heterogeneity, the outcome regression $\gamma_0(D,Z)$ depends on both treatment $D$ and covariates $Z$, so the plug-in score error $\xi(D,Z) = \gamma_0(D,Z) - \hat{\gamma}(D,Z)$ contains treatment-specific components that cannot belong to the linear space spanned by covariate-only basis functions $\tilde{\Phi}(Z)$. As Kato (2026) demonstrates, balancing only functions of $Z$ leaves this treatment-specific score error unbalanced, preventing the first-order cancellation required for true Neyman orthogonality. By instead enforcing regressor balancing via Riesz regression on basis functions $\Phi(D,Z)$, Kato eliminates the represented component of $\xi(D,Z)$, which directly satisfies the directional derivative condition $\mathcal{D}_g S_\theta(\theta^\star, g_0)[g - g_0] = 0$ formalized by Yu et al. (2025) and ensures that downstream parameter updates achieve fourth-power $\|\hat{g}-g_0\|_G^4$ error insensitivity rather than suffering first-order bias.

ground_truth.answer_text: In linear structural causal models with latent confounding ($\Sigma = (I - \Lambda)^{-\top} \Omega (I - \Lambda)^{-1}$), deciding rational identifiability via standard Gröbner basis elimination suffers from doubly exponential complexity $O(2^{2^{|V|}})$, which Hollering et al. (2026) overcome using a quasi-polynomial algorithm that extracts lowest-degree rational identification formulas. However, even when a causal effect is rationally identified, naively substituting first-stage empirical covariance or nuisance estimates into the identification formula yields first-order bias and non-Gaussian asymptotic errors. To ensure robust statistical estimation downstream, the identified parameter must be formulated via a Neyman-orthogonal score or orthogonalized stochastic gradient update (Yu et al., 2025; Kato, 2026), which eliminates first-order sensitivity to nuisance estimation errors and allows flexible machine-learning estimation of intermediate components.

ground_truth.answer_text: First, as shown by Shao et al. (2506.14950, Section 1 & 2), naively plugging in deep neural network first-stage nuisance fits into second-stage CMR estimation induces severe regularization and overfitting bias, requiring an orthogonalized DML score (DML-CMR) to achieve minimax optimal O(N^{-1/2}) convergence. Second, Jin et al. (2507.02275, Section 1 & 3.2) prove that standard first-order DML is minimax rate-optimal only under the 'Gaussian treatment barrier'; when treatment noise is independent and non-Gaussian, standard DML is strictly suboptimal, requiring Structure-Agnostic Cumulant Estimators (ACE) that exploit higher-order cumulants (kappa_{r+1} != 0) to achieve r-th order insensitivity to nuisance errors epsilon (O(epsilon^r + N^{-1/2})). Third, Li et al. (2507.12435, Section 1 & 2) resolve the optimization bottleneck for second-order/targeted debiasing in overparameterized neural networks where Hessian condition C5 fails: rather than relying on Hessian inversion (like autoDML) or post-hoc fluctuations, Targeted Deep Architectures (TDA) projects influence functions directly onto neural network loss gradients nabla_theta ell, achieving double robustness and semiparametric efficiency without Hessian regularity.

ground_truth.answer_text: In longitudinal settings with unobserved unit heterogeneity, Fuhr & Papies (2409.01266, Section 1) demonstrate that applying standard cross-sectional Double Machine Learning (DML) fails because unobserved heterogeneity is often non-additively separable and correlates with observed nonlinear confounders, requiring a Correlated Random Effects (CRE / Mundlak 1978) specification inside DML. Similarly, when estimating multidimensional causal overlap effects over a shared life course, He & Elwert (2607.04567, Section 1) show that unobserved contextual traits act simultaneously as unobserved confounders and as integral components of the overlap treatment effect, causing conventional fixed effects to be biased and requiring a Cumulative Fixed Effects (CFE) estimator on three-wave panel data. Finally, when multi-period treatment effects are identified via recursive regressions of generated outcomes, Rutgers & Singh (2606.29009, Section 1) prove that under ridge penalization, the doubly robust estimator decays geometrically in the weight assigned to recursive OLS across time periods, demonstrating that standard cross-sectional DR intuition applies less and less as time horizons lengthen.

ground_truth.answer_text: Standard individual-level causal estimators and unadjusted variance calculations fail under interference because cross-unit interactions induce statistical dependence and bias target parameter definitions. In sequential market or algorithmic settings, Hays & Raghavan (2504.08836, Section 1 & 2) overcome SUTVA violations by assuming potential outcomes are independent conditional on a shared state, extending Double Machine Learning (DML) to efficiently estimate the Average Direct Effect (ADE) and Global Average Treatment Effect (GATE). In group interaction experiments where individuals are randomly allocated into interacting groups, Fu, Samii, & Wang (2607.02385, Section 1) prove that under a sparse-sampling asymptotic regime, individual-level heteroskedasticity-robust inference is invalid under interference, whereas cluster-robust inference remains design-consistent for marginalized exposure effects. Furthermore, when complex multi-parameter target functionals under such structured data generating processes are modeled with deep neural networks, Li et al. (2507.12435, Section 2) show that embedding TMLE directly into neural weight space via projection along targeting loss gradients removes first-order bias without requiring cumbersome post-hoc models.

ground_truth.answer_text: Standard prediction intervals and aggregate causal summaries fail under sample selection or distributional sign discontinuities because observed calibration samples misrepresent target counterfactual laws and mean summaries average out opposing treatment effects. Under one-sided monotone sample selection, Lee (2607.02898, Section 1 & 2) shows that selected treated units are a mixture of always-selected and marginal-in units, causing ordinary conformal prediction to under-cover; Conformalized Lee Inference resolves this by shifting the prediction set cutoff using the observed treatment-control selection gap. When analyzing distributional treatment effects (DTEs) where marginal effects switch signs along the outcome distribution, Antognini & Verme (2606.28017, Section 1 & 2) demonstrate that Treatment Effects Curves (TECs) require Horizontal Discontinuity Analysis (HDA) via causal forests to compare opposite-signed regions and Vertical Discontinuity Analysis (VDA) with crossing-point asymptotics and bias-corrected Wald statistics to test zero-crossing non-tangentiality.

ground_truth.answer_text: The three frameworks eliminate nuisance estimation bias through distinct mathematical mechanisms: first-order moment orthogonalization, higher-order cumulant cancellation, and parameter-space influence projection. For conditional moment restrictions estimated via deep neural networks, Shao et al. (2506.14950, Section 1 & 2) construct a first-order Neyman-orthogonal score function combined with cross-fitting (DML-CMR) to cancel first-order regularization and overfitting bias, achieving minimax optimal O(N^{-1/2}) convergence. When first-order DML remains suboptimal due to non-Gaussian treatment noise, Jin, Mackey, & Syrgkanis (2507.02275, Section 1 & 4) construct Structure-Agnostic Cumulant Estimators (ACE) that achieve r-th order orthogonality (O(epsilon^r + N^{-1/2}) rates) whenever the (r+1)-st treatment cumulant is non-zero. Conversely, rather than modifying moment equations, Li et al. (2507.12435, Section 1 & 2) propose Targeted Deep Architectures (TDA), which embeds TMLE directly into neural weight space by iteratively updating a targeting parameter subset along a gradient derived from projecting influence functions onto loss gradients nabla_theta ell, achieving double robustness and semiparametric efficiency even for multi-parameter targets.

ground_truth.answer_text: When nuisance functions have Hölder smoothness index s/d in (0.25, 0.5], classical first-order influence function estimators and standard double machine learning suffer from second-order bias terms that decay slower than O(n^{-1/2}), preventing root-n consistency and asymptotic normality. Zhang, Liu, and Zhang (2026) overcome this in non-separable structural models and QTE estimation by constructing higher-order influence functions (HOIF) and U-process debiased estimators that explicitly remove higher-order bias terms, achieving rate-optimal n^{-1/2}-consistency under the minimal smoothness condition s/d > 0.25. Conversely, Sun and Zhang (2026) address unmeasured confounding via a Triple Machine Learning (TML) cross-fitting estimator that sequentially handles representation learning, nuisance functions, and target functionals; however, their asymptotic theory demonstrates that representation learning error induces variance inflation, requiring super-convergence of the representation learner to avoid first-stage bias propagation and achieve semiparametric efficiency.

ground_truth.answer_text: Luo and Liu (2026) point-identify structural causal functions by assuming the instrument shifts treatment independently of latent confounders and substituting the confounded outcome likelihood with an IV-integrated pseudo-likelihood that averages over instrument-induced treatment distributions in the latent space. Similarly, Sun and Zhang (2026) prove nonparametric point identification of the latent confounding subspace by imposing completeness and injectivity conditions on the instrument-to-treatment mapping, using an IV-VAE with an HSIC penalty to ensure latent representations remain exogenous to the instrument. In contrast, Dong and Heiler (2026) show that when outcome observability is subject to non-random sample selection alongside treatment noncompliance, point identification of complier treatment effects fails even with a valid instrument; they instead derive sharp nonparametric bounds for intensive margin effects among always-selected compliers under weak sample selection monotonicity.

ground_truth.answer_text: Standard TWFE and mean-based DiD fail when interventions alter outcome dispersion, tails, or conditional quantiles without shifting mean expectations, or when panel attrition depends non-ignorably on outcome levels. Bhattacharjee, Li, and Xue (2026) replace additive mean parallel trends with an optimal transport (OT) map that transports the control group's pre-to-post distributional drift onto the treated group's baseline distribution, detecting general distributional treatment effects via an RKHS Maximum Mean Discrepancy test. Wied (2026) formulates parallel trends directly within the parameter space of a semiparametric distribution regression (DR) model constrained by synthetic control weights, capturing conditional distributional impacts across covariates. Conversely, Li and Mu (2026) resolve MNAR outcome attrition by introducing a fully observed shadow variable—conditionally independent of missingness given outcome evolution—allowing semiparametric identification of the ATT without requiring missing-at-random assumptions.

ground_truth.answer_text: Ad-hoc discretization—such as sorting policy effects into equally spaced quantiles (GATES) or fixing structural parameters by convention—can distort policy summaries and introduce severe calibration bias. Montiel Olea et al. (2026) prove that under a weighted MSE decision criterion, optimal archetype discovery partitions heterogeneous policy effects via weighted K-means clustering of the scalar effects (or their posterior means), solvable in O(KN^2) time via dynamic programming. Alegre Canton (2026) treats the calibration-estimation split in structural models as a partition-selection problem that minimizes a scalar local sensitivity statistic, selecting the partition with minimal worst-case local bias under calibration error. Meanwhile, Jun and Zincenko (2026) model unobserved confounders through discrete finite mixture types, establishing that bounding the number of latent types below a linear cutoff yields sharp ATE identified sets strictly tighter than worst-case Manski bounds.

ground_truth.answer_text: Without unconfoundedness or instrumental variables, Jun and Zincenko (2026) show that the ATE is only partially identified under discrete unobserved confounding, deriving sharp finite-mixture bounds that are strictly tighter than Manski bounds whenever the number of latent types lies below a linear threshold. Similarly, Dong and Heiler (2026) demonstrate that even with an instrument, simultaneous treatment noncompliance and non-ignorable sample selection prevent point identification of complier treatment effects, necessitating sharp partial identification bounds under weak sample selection monotonicity. In contrast, Sun and Zhang (2026) establish full nonparametric point identification of the latent confounding subspace by leveraging instrumental variables under operator injectivity and completeness conditions, allowing their Triple Machine Learning (TML) procedure to recover marginal counterfactual distributions without relying on partial identification bounds.
